coredata.dc:title: Enhanced state of health estimation for lithium-ion batteries using statistical feature extraction and feature selection
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of ESG.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of ESG.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Shao-Ying
Liu, Yi-Hua
Liu, Chun-Liang
Chen, Guan-Jhu
Wang, Shun-Chung
coredata.dc:description: State of health (SOH) estimation remains a critical research focus in battery management systems, where feature extraction and selection play crucial roles in improving estimation accuracy. This study examines the effectiveness of statistical feature extraction from constant current-constant voltage (CC-CV) charging curves, focusing on voltage and current characteristics. A total of 50 health indicators (HIs) are derived, including several novel features introduced for the first time. Five fundamental machine learning models—including backpropagation neural networks (BPNN), regression trees (RT), and three types of linear regression (Ridge, Lasso, and Elastic Net)—are trained on these features, with hyperparameter optimization conducted via random search. The best-performing model, RT, is further refined through seven feature selection techniques. Experimental results demonstrate that selecting only the top five features using sequential feature selection (backward) (SFS_backward) and recursive feature elimination (RFE) significantly enhances performance. Compared to using all features, SFS_backward and RFE reduce root mean square error (RMSE) by 12.8 % and 12.5 %, respectively, while mean absolute error (MAE) decreases by 13.1 % and 15.2 %. The proposed methodology also outperforms conventional and deep learning approaches, achieving up to a 127.0 % reduction in RMSE and 113.3 % in MAE. These findings underscore the potential of statistical feature engineering and selection to enhance SOH estimation accuracy while reducing model complexity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: State of Health (SOH) Estimation
Feature Selection
Machine Learning
CC-CV Charging Curves
Lithium-ion Batteries

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Extracted waveforms from NASA dataset (a) B05 (b) B06 (c) B07 (d) B18.
Degradation path of all the batteries (B05, B06, B07, and B18).
The correlation matrix of all extracted features listed in Table 2 and SOH value.
The predicted versus actual plot for all the compared methods (a) LR methods (b) BPNN (c) RT.
Feature importance metrics for all the compared LR methods (a) Lasso (b) Ridge (c) Elastic Net.
Performance comparison of all the feature selection methods.
The predicted versus actual plots for the best-performing model using the top five selected features and that using all features.
Extracted statistical features and their definitions.

row.entry: Average value of the time series.
Middle value when data is orderd.
Standard deviation of disperison around the mean.
Value separating the lowest 25 % of data.
Value separating the highest 25 % of data.
Measures spread of the middle 50 % of data.
Sum of absolute differences between consecutive points.
Mean of differences between consecutive points.
Maximum absolute difference between consecutive points.
Number of points exceeding the mean.
Proportion of points above the mean.
First lag where autocorrelation falls below statistical significance.
Determined via ACF plots with confidence intervals.
Partial autocorrelation (direct correlation between points at lag k).
Entrophy after discretizing data into 10/30 bins.

−
∑
i = 1
B
P
i
log
P
i
, B = number of bins.
Regularity quantification (logarithmic likelihood of similar paterns).
Similar to approximate entropy but excludes self-matches.
Length of the time series (seconds or samples).

N × Δ t
, where
Δ t
is the sampling interval.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Search ranges for the hyperparameters and the final optimized values.
The pros and cons of the utilized feature selection methods.

row.entry: Simple, fast, and computationally efficient; suitable for high-dimensional data.
Ignores feature interactions; relies solely on univariate statistics, potentially missing complex relationships.
Flexible with percentage-based selection; quick and easy to implement.
Similar to SKB, it overlooks feature dependencies and may select redundant features.
Versatile with multiple modes (e.g., k-best, percentile); easily customizable.
Limited by univariate approach; may not capture multivariate feature importance.
Considers feature interactions via a wrapper approach; can improve model performance.
Computationally expensive, especially with large feature sets; sensitive to the base estimator.
Effectively prunes irrelevant features; accounts for interactions like forward SFS.
High computational cost; may remove useful features early, depending on the estimator.
Embedded method leveraging regularization; effectively handles multicollinearity.
Sensitive to alpha value; may discard features with small but meaningful contributions.
Captures non-linear relationships and feature importance; robust to noise.
Computationally intensive; importance scores can be biased toward correlated features.
Systematically ranks features; works well with any estimator.
Slow for large datasets; recursive nature increases computation time; depends on estimator quality.
Optimizes feature selection with cross-validation; reduces overfitting risk.
Most computationally demanding; requires sufficient data for reliable cross-validation.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Search ranges for the hyperparameters and the final optimized values.
SOH estimation comparison with other state-of-the-art methods.

row.entry: Utilized battery database and the extracted features
NASA/Statistical features from CC and CV section

NASA/Discharge related data selected by DT

head.ce:title: Enhanced state of health estimation for lithium-ion batteries using statistical feature extraction and feature selection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electrical Engineering, National Taiwan University of Science and Technology, Taipei 106335, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Electrical Engineering, National Taiwan University of Science and Technology

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Electrical Engineering, National Yunlin University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Marine Engineering, National Taiwan Ocean University, Keelung 202301, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Marine Engineering, National Taiwan Ocean University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Marine Engineering, National Taiwan Ocean University, Keelung 202301, Taiwan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: State of health (SOH) estimation remains a critical research focus in battery management systems, where feature extraction and selection play crucial roles in improving estimation accuracy. This study examines the effectiveness of statistical feature extraction from constant current-constant voltage (CC-CV) charging curves, focusing on voltage and current characteristics. A total of 50 health indicators (HIs) are derived, including several novel features introduced for the first time. Five fundamental machine learning models—including backpropagation neural networks (BPNN), regression trees (RT), and three types of linear regression (Ridge, Lasso, and Elastic Net)—are trained on these features, with hyperparameter optimization conducted via random search. The best-performing model, RT, is further refined through seven feature selection techniques. Experimental results demonstrate that selecting only the top five features using sequential feature selection (backward) (SFS_backward) and recursive feature elimination (RFE) significantly enhances performance. Compared to using all features, SFS_backward and RFE reduce root mean square error (RMSE) by 12.8 % and 12.5 %, respectively, while mean absolute error (MAE) decreases by 13.1 % and 15.2 %. The proposed methodology also outperforms conventional and deep learning approaches, achieving up to a 127.0 % reduction in RMSE and 113.3 % in MAE. These findings underscore the potential of statistical feature engineering and selection to enhance SOH estimation accuracy while reducing model complexity.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries have become indispensable in electric vehicles and energy storage systems due to their high energy density, long cycle life, and low self-discharge rates. However, aging-induced performance degradation and thermal runaway can pose serious hazards, making accurate SOH estimation crucial for battery management and safety assessment [1]. SOH estimation techniques can be classified into four main categories: Direct measurement methods involve experimental tests to directly assess battery parameters. One such technique is Coulomb counting, which measures battery capacity by tracking charge and discharge cycles [2]. Another widely adopted approach is electrochemical impedance spectroscopy, which provides insights into battery health through impedance analysis [3]. Additionally, internal resistance measurement using pulse current tests offers a practical approach for evaluating battery degradation [4]. Model-based methods rely on theoretical or empirical models to estimate battery state. Electrochemical models provide highly accurate estimations but are complex to implement [5]. equivalent circuit models (ECMs) offer a balance between accuracy and computational efficiency, making them a widely adopted approach [6]. Semi-empirical models integrate experimental data with theoretical frameworks to enhance estimation accuracy while maintaining feasibility [7]. Data-driven methods utilize machine learning and artificial intelligence to infer battery health based on historical data. Traditional machine learning techniques, such as Gaussian process regression (GPR), regression trees and support vector machines, are commonly employed for SOH estimation [8]. More advanced approaches leverage deep learning models, including long short-term memory (LSTM) networks and convolutional neural networks (CNNs), to identify complex patterns in battery behavior [9]. Transfer learning has also been explored to improve generalization across different battery types and operating conditions [10]. Hybrid methods combine multiple approaches to leverage their complementary advantages. Model-data fusion techniques integrate theoretical models with data-driven learning for enhanced estimation accuracy [11]. Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) incorporate domain knowledge into deep learning architectures, improving interpretability and robustness [12]. Additionally, multi-model parallel prediction frameworks utilize multiple estimation models concurrently to achieve higher reliability in SOH assessment [13]. Table 1 summarizes the advantages and disadvantages of various SOH estimation methods.

From Table 1, data-driven methods are increasingly preferred in battery research due to their ability to eliminate complex physical modeling, offer superior adaptability and flexibility while effectively handling nonlinear relationships and complex aging patterns. These methods enable real-time estimation with a lower computational burden and do not require expensive testing equipment, unlike direct measurement methods. For data-driven methods, selecting appropriate features significantly impacts model performance. Several studies have investigated HIs used for SOH estimation. Li et al. [14] initially selected 28 health features from three perspectives: cumulative features, operating state features, and individual consistency features, which were subsequently refined to the top 10 most relevant features based on correlation analysis. Lee et al. [15] employed a window-moving technique to extract statistical features representing battery capacity-fading behaviors over specific cycles, incorporating moving average, moving variance, and the first-order difference of the SOH. Zhang et al. [16] extracted HIs from voltage-capacity curves during battery charging, presenting them as an alternative to incremental capacity (IC) curves due to IC curve feature extraction limitations. This study demonstrated that voltage-capacity curves exhibit greater feature correlation than voltage-time curves. Lee et al. [17] converted capacity degradation data into two-dimensional images using recurrence plots and Gramian angular fields, effectively capturing time-series characteristics without relying on predefined statistical features. Cai et al. [18] utilized knee points in the voltage response of a short-term current pulse test as features for SOH estimation, which can be readily obtained from the power converter controller in real-world applications. He and Gong [19] proposed a multi-scale convolutional attention mechanism to automatically capture and enhance features correlated with SOH decay from charging data, eliminating the need for manual feature extraction. Zhang et al. [20] introduced a novel battery SOH estimation method for partial charging segments, utilizing an incremental energy per state of charge (SOC) curve and a bidirectional LSTM with a reduction mechanism. The method ensures reliability and feasibility, achieving an RMSE below 0.8 % and R2 values exceeding 0.9 across different charging rates. Finally, Etxandi-Santolaya et al. [21] focused on partial charge-based His by leveraging the voltage range encompassing the second and third IC peaks. This study further employed the energy variation index and IC analysis as His, while using full charge data to identify voltage ranges most sensitive to degradation.

In contrast, utilizing voltage, current, and temperature curves from CC and/or CV segments as HIs is a widely adopted technique in data-driven SOH estimation methods. Both CC and CV segments offer distinct advantages for SOH estimation. CC curves benefit from a controlled charging process, ease of measurement, and a direct reflection of battery degradation, while CV curves are robust, sensitive to aging, and practical for partial charging, offering a variety of health-related features. The choice between these approaches often depends on the specific application, data availability, and the battery type being analyzed. Several studies have explored health indicators extracted from different segments of the battery charging process, with a focus on CC and CV phases. Maity et al. [22] examined multiple features derived from the CV charging phase, including CV duration, time to reach specific current values, total energy in CV, and other features extracted from current profiles. This study identified the time required to reach a specific current as the most dominant health indicator. Yang et al. [23] focused on the time constant of the CV charging current, and CV charging time as key parameters related to battery capacity fading. Chen et al. [24] used the duration of CV charging, along with the Shannon entropy of the CV charging duration sequence and the increment sequence, as health indicators. Zhang et al. [25] extracted five feature indices from the CV stage of the CC-CV charging curve, using the slope index as a key health factor, identified through variance inflation factor analysis. Additionally, Zhang et al. [26] utilized data from the CV stage to extract features such as charging duration, time-integral of the current, and the chi-square value of current. Guo et al. [27] employed the maximum value of the IC curve, the corresponding voltage, energy, and capacity of a CC charging interval determined by the IC curve’s maximum value as health indicators. Wang et al. [28] extracted statistical features from a short period of data before the battery reaches full charge, including mean, standard deviation, kurtosis, skewness, charging time, accumulated charge, curve slope, and curve entropy from current and voltage curves. Guo et al. [29] proposed five features based on voltage and current during the charging stage, and applied the median absolute deviation Savitzky–Golay filtering method to improve correlation with battery capacity. Maures et al. [30] utilized IC peak tracking during charge and discharge cycles at different C-rates as a SOH estimator. Eddahech et al. [31] investigated CV charge phase data, leveraging CV current and/or CV duration as aging-related health indicators. Guo et al. [32] employed a benchmark charging profile as a basis for SOH estimation. Given these advantages, this study also utilizes voltage and current waveforms from the CC and CV segments to extract HIs for SOH estimation.

On the other hand, feature selection methods are crucial for data-driven approaches, as they identify the most relevant input variables (features) that impact model accuracy while reducing model complexity. By selecting the most effective features, these methods enhance accuracy, efficiency, and reliability. Feature selection methods can be categorized into three main types: filter methods, wrapper methods, and fusion methods. Filter methods evaluate the correlation between each feature and the target variable using statistical measures, without involving a machine learning algorithm. Wang et al. [33] used Pearson correlation coefficients (PCCs) to analyze the linear relationship between features and capacity, while Li et al. [34] employed grey relation analysis to quantify the correlation between features and battery SOH. Greenbank and Howey [35] also used PCCs to measure the similarity between features and changes in battery SOH. Wrapper methods use a specific learning algorithm to evaluate the quality of feature subsets. In Hu et al. [36], the sequential backward search method was implemented for subset selection. Wang et al. [37] used a random forest model to derive feature importance scores, which were then used to assign initial weights to the features. Fusion methods integrate filter and wrapper approaches to enhance feature selection. Hu et al. [36] combined PCCs to filter out unimportant features and then uses SBS to remove redundant features. Similarly, Zhang et al. [38] extracted features from multiple domains (time, frequency, entropy, and time-series), used the Gini coefficient to calculate feature significance, and then employed multi-set discriminant correlation analysis for feature fusion. Wang et al. [37] used a random forest model to derive feature importance scores and then applied an enhanced whale optimization algorithm to select features, combining filter-based importance with an optimization algorithm.

Furthermore, hybrid and physics-informed machine learning techniques have emerged as promising approaches for SOH estimation of lithium-ion batteries. These methods leverage the strengths of both data-driven models and physics-based battery models to achieve improved accuracy, interpretability, and reliability in SOH prediction. For hybrid methods, Zhang et al. [39] introduced a novel SOH estimation method for lithium-ion batteries using a hybrid model combining Kolmogorov–Arnold Networks and LSTM. The method extracts features from fully charged battery characteristics (voltage, temperature, charging data) and demonstrates highly accurate SOH estimation under varying conditions, achieving low MAE and RMSE and high R2 values on both proprietary and NASA datasets. Chen et al. [40] proposed a new SOH estimation method that integrates an ECM with a deep learning network. This approach uses correlation analysis to select health features from battery data and ECM parameters identified through a hybrid optimization strategy. An improved vision transformer network is then trained with these features, achieving higher prediction accuracy compared to other methods on NASA and CALCE datasets. Additionally, Zhang et al. [41] presented a multi-task learning framework using a Convolutional Neural Network-Multi-gate Mixture of Gated Recurrent Units (CMMOG) for lithium-ion battery SOH estimation. The method extracts health indicators using IC analysis and fuzzy entropy. Experimental results show that the proposed model achieves faster convergence and improved generalization across different operating conditions and battery samples, outperforming single-task learning methods. For physics-informed techniques, Ye et al. [42] proposed a PINN to enhance the interpretability and accuracy of lithium-ion battery SOH estimation. Features extracted from IC and differential temperature curves are used as input, while physical constraints based on the monotonic relationship between peak IC and SOH are incorporated into the neural network training. Secondary training with physical constraints further improves prediction accuracy, validated using Oxford and NASA battery datasets, achieving errors below 1.5 % on SOH estimation. Wen et al. [43] presented a PINN framework for prognostics and health management of lithium-ion batteries. This framework fuses semi-physics and semi-empirical dynamic models with data-driven discovered dynamics and neural networks. An uncertainty-based method adaptively weighs multi-task losses during training. The proposed methods are validated on a public dataset, demonstrating improved performance in SOH estimation, and remaining useful life prediction compared to baseline models. Wang et al. [28] introduced a PINN for accurate and stable estimation of lithium-ion battery SOH. It models degradation dynamics using empirical degradation and state-space equations, which are learned by neural networks. A general feature extraction method based on a short period of charging data is proposed, making it applicable to diverse battery types and protocols. Validated on a comprehensive dataset of 387 batteries, the PINN outperforms conventional methods in regular, small-sample, and transfer learning experiments, achieving a mean absolute percentage error of 0.87 %. Lastly, Zhang et al. [44] presented a multi-fault diagnosis methodology for lithium-ion battery packs, aimed at enhancing safety and reliability in electric vehicles. The methodology utilizes a combination of curvilinear Manhattan distance and voltage difference analysis to swiftly identify and locate faults within battery cells. The findings suggest that by combining fault diagnosis with SOH assessment, this approach could lead to more robust monitoring systems for lithium-ion batteries, ultimately enhancing the safety and longevity of electric vehicles.

From the above discussion, it is evident that SOH estimation remains one of the most widely studied topics in battery research. Extracting appropriate features and selecting the most representative ones can significantly impact SOH estimation performance. This study focuses on the commonly used CC-CV charging curves and applies various statistical methods to extract statistical summary data from the CC-segment voltage and CV-segment current, resulting in a total of 50 health indicators (HIs). To the best of the authors' knowledge, some of these features are presented for the first time in the literature. Next, five fundamental machine learning models—including BPNN, RT, and three types of linear regression: Ridge, Lasso, and Elastic Net—are trained on all 50 HIs, with hyperparameter optimization performed using the random search method. After obtaining the optimal models, this study further applies seven different feature selection methods to the best-performing RT model for feature selection and compares their performance. The experimental results demonstrate that selecting only the top five features enables both SFS_backward and RFE to effectively reduce model complexity while achieving higher estimation accuracy. Compared to using all features, SFS_backward and RFE improve RMSE by 12.8 % and 12.5 %, respectively, while MAE improves by 13.1 % and 15.2 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Data source and the extracted health indicators
ce:section.ce:para: In lithium-ion battery charging processes, two critical operational phases are CC charging and CV charging. During these phases, time-series data—such as CCV (voltage waveform during CC charging) and CVI (current waveform during CV charging) —are generated. These datasets capture dynamic electrochemical behaviors and degradation patterns, making them valuable for battery health monitoring. This section describes the extraction of 25 statistical features from each waveform (F01–F25 for CCV; F26–F50 for CVI), along with their definitions and computation methodologies. This study utilizes the publicly available NASA battery dataset, specifically focusing on cells B05, B06, B07, and B18. The NASA battery dataset is widely recognized for its comprehensive and high-quality data, which includes detailed records of battery performance under various operational conditions, such as charge-discharge cycles, capacity fade, and impedance measurements [45]. One of the key advantages of using this dataset is its accessibility and reproducibility, enabling researchers to conduct rigorous analyses and comparisons. The dataset encompasses critical parameters, including voltage, current, temperature, and cycle life, which are essential for understanding battery degradation mechanisms and developing predictive models. By leveraging this dataset, our study aims to contribute to the advancement of battery health management and performance optimization. Fig. 1 presents the extracted waveforms from NASA dataset, while Fig. 2 illustrates the battery degradation data for all tested cells. As shown in Fig. 1, the CCV curve exhibits systematic variations as the battery ages. During the constant-current phase, the battery voltage increases as it accumulates charge under a fixed current. The voltage response (CCV) is sensitive to internal resistance changes, capacity fade, and other degradation phenomena. During the constant-voltage phase, the voltage remains constant, while the current gradually decreases as the battery approaches full charge. The current behavior (CVI) reflects the battery’s ability to sustain its voltage under diminishing current, which is influenced by electrochemical kinetics and degradation effects. The statistical features of CCV and CVI capture various aspects of the CC and CV charging curves, including voltage levels, current levels, rates of change, and temporal correlations. These features are sensitive to changes in the battery's internal resistance, capacity, and other electrochemical properties, which are all indicators of SOH. By analyzing these statistical features, it is possible to develop models that can accurately estimate the SOH of a battery. In essence, the changes in the shape and characteristics of the CC and CV charging curves, represented by these statistical features, provide valuable information about the battery's degradation state. Therefore, extracting statistical features from the CCV curve serves as an effective approach for SOH estimation. The extracted statistical features and their definitions are summarized in Table 2. Among the statistical features listed in Table 2, those derived from basic statistical methods (row 1–14, row 25) are commonly used in advanced SOH estimation studies. However, the variability and distribution features (row 15–19) and the autocorrelation features (row 20, 21) are proposed for the first time in this study. Regarding the entropy-based features (row 22–24), only sample entropy has been previously mentioned in SOH-related literature [46].

Fig. 3 presents the correlation matrix of all extracted features (F01–F50) listed in Table 2, along with the PCCs between each feature and the target SOH. As observed in Fig. 3, the correlation between individual features and SOH varies significantly. Moreover, the inter-feature correlations exhibit distinct relationships, indicating potential dependences among extracted features. These findings emphasize the importance of feature engineering and feature selection in SOH estimation. Effective feature selection can improve model performance by reducing redundancy, mitigating the effects of irrelevant or weakly correlated features, and enhancing computational efficiency. Consequently, identifying an optimal feature subset for accurate SOH estimation remains a critical research challenge.

As described earlier, this study extracts a total of 50 statistical features from CCV and CVI time-series data, which have been demonstrated to be effective for SOH estimation. Next, all extracted statistical features are utilized to compare the SOH estimation performance of five different machine learning methods. The evaluated machine learning methods include BPNN [47], RT [48], and three LR techniques: Lasso, Ridge, and Elastic Net [49]. These methods serve as foundational machine learning approaches for predictive modeling. BPNNs are multi-layer feedforward networks that adjust connection weights via gradient descent to minimize prediction errors. They are particularly effective at capturing nonlinear patterns in complex datasets, such as sensor readings or image data. RTs partition data into subgroups using decision rules (e.g., splits on predictor variables) and assign constant predictions (e.g., mean values) to each terminal node. This approach enhances interpretability, making RTs well-suited for applications such as customer segmentation and medical diagnosis. For LR techniques, Lasso applies L1 regularization to shrink less important coefficients to zero, thereby enabling automatic feature selection. Ridge regression employs L2 regularization to mitigate multicollinearity by penalizing large coefficients, improving model stability. Elastic Net combines L1 and L2 regularization, incorporating the strengths of both Lasso and Ridge to handle correlated predictors and enhance model stability in high-dimensional datasets. These methods address overfitting and improve model generalizability, making them applicable to a wide range of predictive modeling tasks, including financial risk assessment and biomedical research.

To ensure a fair comparison, the hyperparameters of the five machine learning models are optimized using the random search method. The hyperparameter ranges and final optimized values are summarized in Table 3. The hyperparameter search involves tuning five distinct machine learning models: a BPNN, a RT, Lasso regression, Ridge regression, and ElasticNet regression. Each model has a specific set of hyperparameters explored using a random search.
•
For the BPNN, the search optimizes:
•
Hidden layer size, ranging from 1 to 50.
•
Initial learning rate, sampled from a log-uniform distribution between 10⁻⁴ and 1.
•
Activation function, selected from 'relu', 'tanh', or 'logistic'.
•
Optimization solver, chosen from 'adam', 'sgd', or 'lbfgs'.
•
For the RT, the search optimizes:
•
Maximum depth, sampled from a discrete uniform distribution between 1 and 20.
•
Minimum samples required to split, ranging from 2 to 20.
•
Minimum samples per leaf, ranging from 1 and 20.
•
Maximum number of features, selected from 'auto', 'sqrt', or 'log2'.
•
For Lasso and Ridge regression, the regularization strength is sampled from a logarithmic scale between 10⁻⁴ and 10² over 100 points.
•
For ElasticNet, the search optimizes:
•
the regularization strength, across the same range as Lasso and Ridge.
•
L1 regularization ratio, sampled across nine evenly spaced values from 0.1 to 0.9.
This setup defines a comprehensive search space, tailored to each model’s requirements. The choice of random search as the optimization method is both practical and justified, given the complexity and dimensionality of the hyperparameter spaces. Random search involves sampling hyperparameter combinations randomly from the defined ranges, rather than exhaustively testing all possibilities (as in grid search). Random search is particularly advantageous because it balances exploration and computational efficiency. Bergstra and Bengio [50] demonstrated that random search can outperform grid search in high-dimensional spaces, particularly when only a few hyperparameters significantly impact performance. In this case, the diverse models and parameter types—continuous, discrete, and categorical—suggest that not all parameters are equally influential, making random search an ideal choice. While random search may not guarantee finding the absolute optimal combination, it often identifies near-optimal solutions faster than grid search, justifying its use for hyperparameter tuning across all specified models.
Additionally, the predicted versus actual plot for all compared methods is displayed in Fig. 4. Since Lasso, Ridge, and Elastic Net regression techniques provide feature importance metrics, their results are also presented in Fig. 5 to facilitate comparative analysis. This visualization enables cross-examination of how different regularization techniques prioritize features, offering complementary insights into the relative significance of variables in the model. The performance metrics used in this study for evaluation are RMSE and MAE, which are briefly defined by Eqs. (1) and (2) respectively:
(1)
R M S E =
1 N
∑
i = 1
N
(
y
i
−
y
i
∧
)
2
•
RMSE computes the square root of the average squared differences between predicted and actual values. Since errors are squared before averaging, RMSE penalizes larger errors more heavily, making it sensitive to outliers.
(2)
M A E =
1 N
∑
i = 1
N
|
y
i
−
y
i
∧
|
•
MAE computes the average absolute differences between predicted and actual values, treating all errors equally. Unlike RMSE, MAE is less sensitive to outliers, making it a robust performance measure.

While RMSE is more suitable for applications where large prediction deviations are critical (e.g., financial risk models), MAE provides an intuitive, scale-matched error measure (e.g., inventory demand forecasting). As shown in Table 3, the RT model achieves the best performance metrics for the given features and dataset. Therefore, in the following analysis, the optimized RT model is used for feature selection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the feature selection methods
ce:section.ce:para: Feature selection is a crucial component of machine learning, enhancing model performance by identifying and retaining informative variables while discarding irrelevant ones. This approach improves model accuracy, reduces overfitting, accelerates training and inference, enhances interpretability, mitigates multicollinearity, simplifies data visualization, and optimizes resource efficiency. Feature selection is particularly beneficial in high-dimensional datasets, such as genomic studies, where thousands of features may obscure true signals. When combined with techniques such as regularization or decision trees, feature selection helps bridge the gap between theoretical models and practical performance.

In this study, the following feature selection methods are implemented and compared: SelectKBest (SKB), SelectPercentile (SP), GenericUnivariateSelect (GUS), SequentialFeatureSelector (SFS), SelectFromModel (SFM), RFE, and Recursive Feature Elimination with Cross-Validation (RFECV).
•
SKB is a filter-based method that selects the top k features based on univariate statistical tests, such as chi-squared, ANOVA F-test, or mutual information. It scores each feature independently and retains the highest-scoring features, making it effective for identifying the most relevant features for a target variable.
•
SP follows the same principle as SKB but selects a percentage of the top-performing features instead of a fixed number, which is useful when the exact number of features to retain is unknown.
•
GUS is a flexible filter-based method that generalizes the approaches of SKB and SP. It allows customization of the scoring function and selection strategy, providing greater control over the feature selection process. This method is particularly beneficial when specific statistical tests or selection criteria are required.
•
SFS iteratively adds (forward selection) or removes (backward elimination) features based on model performance. It evaluates features subsets using cross-validation and selects the best-performing subset, making it ideal for identifying the optimal feature set for a specific machine learning model.
•
SFM is an embedded method that selects features based on importance weights derived from a trained model, such as linear models or tree-based models. It applies a threshold to retain features with importance scores above a certain value, leveraging the model's inherent ability to evaluate feature relevance.
•
RFE is a wrapper method that recursively removes the least important features based on model weights or coefficients. It trains the model, eliminates the least significant feature, and repeats the process until the desired number of features is reached. This method is particularly useful for reducing the feature space while maintaining model performance.
•
RFECV extends RFE by incorporating cross-validation to determine the optimal number of features. It performs RFE while evaluating model performance at each step using cross-validation, providing a more robust approach to feature selection.

These methods encompass filter-based, wrapper-based, and embedded approaches, each with unique strengths and applications, enabling data scientists to choose the most appropriate technique based on their specific needs and dataset characteristics. Table 4 illustrates the pros and cons of these feature selection methods.

Table 5 presents the performance of all feature selection methods when the number of selected features is set to five. Fig. 6 further compares the RMSE and MAE of the best SOH estimation results obtained from each feature selection method with those of the best-performing model using all features. Additionally, the corresponding runtime (RT) of the best-performing model with all features is included for comparison. To ensure fairness, each feature selection method is executed ten times, and the results are averaged. In this study, the configuration of these feature selection method is as follows:
•
SKB selects the top 5 features based on the highest scores from the f_regression scoring function (the F-statistic and corresponding p-values).
•
SP picks the top 10 % of features using the same scoring function.
•
GUS is configured to select the 5 best features with f_regression.
•
Sequential approaches include SFS_forward and SFS_backward, both using a decision tree regressor (with predefined parameters) to iteratively select five features, either by adding features (forward selection) or removing features (backward elimination).
•
Model-based methods include SFM_Lasso, which leverages a Lasso regression model with alpha= 0.01 to select up to five features, and SFM_RF, which employs a random forest regressor with 100 estimators to identify up to five key features.
•
Recursive methods include RFE, which recursively eliminates features using a decision tree regressor until five remain, and RFECV, an enhanced version that employs cross-validation to determine the optimal number of features, with a minimum of five.

These techniques span univariate statistical methods, wrapper methods, and embedded methods, offering diverse strategies for feature selection. As shown in Table 5, when selecting five features, three methods—SFS_backward, RFE, and RFECV—achieve lower RMSE and MAE compared to using all features, with SFS_backward and RFE demonstrating the best performance. Both methods select four features from CCV, while the CVI feature chosen in both cases is F37. Examining the feature selection results across the nine methods, selecting more CCV features generally leads to better performance (CCV features are highlighted in red in Table 5). However, none of the selected feature sets consist solely of CCV features. Notably, features F37, F38, F40, F46, and F50 are simultaneously selected by SKB, SP, and GUS, indicating that this combination is particularly effective.

Among the features selected by the nine methods, the following 19 features appear: F02 (3 times), F06 (1 time), F09 (1 time), F12 (2 times), F13 (1 time), F14 (1 time), F15 (3 times), F16 (1 time), F18 (2 times), F20 (1 time), F21 (2 times), F25 (2 times), F37 (9 times), F38 (5 times), F40 (4 times), F43 (2 times), F45 (2 times), F46 (3 times), and F50 (5 times). Of those, 12 features belong to CCV, while 7 belong to CVI, with CVI features exhibiting better performance in feature selection refinement. F37 is selected across all methods, followed by F38 and F50, which appear in four different methods (SKB, SP, GUS, and SFS_forward).

Some features are selected only once in specific methods, such as F14 (SFS_backward), F06 (SFM_RF), F20 (RFE), and F09, F13, and F16 (RFECV). Interestingly, three of these methods (SFS_backward, RFE, and RFECV) demonstrate superior performance, suggesting that the quality of the selected feature combination is more critical than the frequency of feature selection. Selecting only the five best features enables both SFS_backward and RFE to effectively reduce model complexity while achieving higher estimation accuracy. The experimental results confirm the effectiveness of feature selection in improving SOH estimation accuracy while reducing model complexity. When selecting only the top five features, the widely used benchmark methods—SFS_backward and RFE—outperform other methods, achieving the lowest RMSE and MAE. Specifically,
•
SFS_backward achieves an RMSE of 0.01588 and an MAE of 0.01050,
•
RFE achieves an RMSE of 0.01594 and an MAE of 0.01024.
In comparison, using all 50 features, which results in an RMSE of 0.01822 and an MAE of 0.01208. thus,
•
SFS_backward improves RMSE by 12.8 % and MAE by 13.1 %,
•
RFE improves RMSE by 12.5 % and MAE by 15.2 %.

The feature selection results indicate that CCV-based features play a dominant role in improving SOH estimation accuracy, with feature F37 being the most frequently selected across multiple methods. The combination of F37, F38, F40, F46, and F50 is consistently chosen by SKB, SP, and GUS, highlighting its effectiveness in SOH prediction. However, the best-performing methods (SFS_backward and RFE) rely on a mix of CCV and CVI features, reinforcing the importance of a diverse feature set. Notably, while RFECV selects 10 features instead of five, its performance does not surpass SFS_backward or RFE, further emphasizing that selecting high-quality features in more critical than merely increasing feature count.

The two best-performing feature selection methods identified the following key features: F02, F12, F14, F15, F18, F20, F21, and F37. The effectiveness of these features is explained as follows:
(1)
Magnitude of charging profiles
•
Features such as the sum (F12 for CCV, F37 for CVI), RMS (F14 for CCV), and the number of points above mean (F18) provide a measure of the overall energy response during charging.
•
These metrics indicate changes in energy distribution, aiding in the tracking of capacity fade and battery degradation.
(2)
Smoothness and fluctuations in voltage signals
•
The sum of absolute differences between consecutive CCV points (F15) reflects signal stability.
•
This feature helps identify irregularities in the voltage profile caused by aging effects.
(3)
Statistical distribution of voltage waveform
•
The median (F02) provides insights into the statistical distribution of the voltage waveform.
•
It helps detect deviations from normal battery behavior, which are critical for SOH estimation.
(4)
Temporal dependencies and battery dynamics
•
The first lag where autocorrelation falls below statistical significance (F20) and partial autocorrelation (F21) capture temporal dependencies in the voltage signal.
•
These features help analyze periodic trends and degradation effects, incorporating the full dynamic response of the battery.

The statistical features derived from the CCV and CVI signals capture critical aspects of the battery’s charging behavior. Their sensitivity to degradation-related changes in the battery's internal dynamics makes them highly suitable for accurately SOH estimation. Fig. 7 presents the predicted versus actual plots for the best-performing model using the top five selected features and the best-performing model using all features. The comparison further highlights the effectiveness of feature selection in enhancing model accuracy while reducing computational complexity.

To further validate the effectiveness of the proposed method, this study compares SOH estimation results with recent advanced methods in the literature. The comparison is conducted in two main categories:
(1)
Studies utilizing CC and CV phase data from the NASA dataset.
(2)
State-of-the-art SOH estimation algorithms from the literature.

For the first category, the features recommended in these studies are extracted, and the same random search optimization process is applied to fine-tune the machine learning models for SOH estimation. The primary objective is to evaluate the impact of different feature sets on SOH estimation accuracy, thereby validating the relevance of the proposed statistical features.
For the second category, the proposed method is compared with cutting-edge SOH estimation algorithms to demonstrate its superiority.

In the first category, the selected features include:
•
Charging time of the CC phase (CCT).
•
Charging time of the CV phase (CVT).
•
Integral of the current in the CV phase (CVQ).
According to Liu et al. [51], these features exhibit a strong correlation with SOH. This study compares three advanced machine learning methods:
•
A BPNN with a maximum of three hidden layers.
•
GPR.
•
support vector regression (SVR).
For the second category, Teng et al. [52] designed a battery data acquisition system to collect comprehensive discharge and charge data from retired lead-acid batteries. The acquired data is then separated into time-interval-based sub-data, from which custom-designed features for SOH estimation are extracted.
Qian et al. [48] extracted features from the NASA database, including:
•
Discharge voltage.
•
Discharge time.
•
Temperature.
•
SOC.
•
Battery voltage at SOC= 100 %.
•
Voltage drop rate.
These features were analyzed using a decision tree algorithm to determine their importance in predicting battery SOH. Rastegarpanah et al. [53] used a library of impedance spectra and corresponding discharge capacities measured from 13 end-of-life Nissan Leaf 2011 battery modules. The extracted features were obtained by fitting the impedance spectra to a modified Randles ECM. These extracted features include:
•
Equivalent series resistance.
•
charge transfer resistance.
•
Warburg resistance.
•
Parameters related to the constant phase element and inductance.

From Table 6, the results highlight the effectiveness of the proposed SOH estimation technique. Compared to conventional machine learning models such as BPNN, GPR, and SVR, the proposed method achieves a 36.5–127.0 % reduction in RMSE and a 68.6–113.3 % reduction in MAE. Additionally, when compared to deep learning approaches such as LSTM and optimized BPNN, the proposed method outperforms them, achieving a 12.6–74.8 % improvement in RMSE and a 14.3–113.3 % improvement in MAE. These results demonstrate that the regression tree model, when combined with appropriately selected statistical features, provides a robust and accurate solution for SOH estimation, outperforming existing methods across various evaluation metrics.

The experimental results confirm that feature selection effectively enhances SOH estimation accuracy while reducing computational complexity. When selecting only the top five features, sequential feature selection (backward) achieves an RMSE of 0.01588 and an MAE of 0.01050, while recursive feature elimination achieves an RMSE of 0.01594 and an MAE of 0.01024. Compared to using all 50 features (RMSE 0.01822, MAE 0.01208), these methods improve RMSE by 12.8 % and 12.5 %, and MAE by 13.1 % and 15.2 %, respectively. Compared to traditional machine learning models such as BPNN, GPR, and SVR, the proposed approach reduces RMSE by 36.5–127.0 % and MAE by 68.6–113.3 %. Furthermore, it outperforms deep learning methods, including LSTM and optimized BPNN, achieving an RMSE improvement of 12.6–74.8 % and an MAE improvement of 14.3–113.3 %. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of statistical feature extraction and optimized feature selection in SOH estimation.

Future research will extend to more publicly available battery databases, such as the CALCE and Oxford battery degradation datasets, or utilize custom test datasets to validate the generalizability and robustness of the proposed statistical features and feature selection method. Additionally, statistical methods will be applied to partial CC/CV curves from real-world test data to reduce data acquisition costs while enhancing SOH estimation accuracy, thereby improving the feasibility and adaptability of the proposed approach in practical applications.

Liu Chun-Liang: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Software, Project administration, Methodology. Liu Yi-Hua: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Li Shao-Ying: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Wang Shun-Chung: Visualization, Validation, Resources, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Chen Guan-Jhu: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Advanced State-of-Health Estimation for Lithium-Ion Batteries Using Multi-Feature Fusion and KAN-LSTM Hybrid Model
